overall.summary: The quiz set is structurally strong, broadly grounded in the chapter, and appropriately foundational for position 2/33. The remaining issues are mostly localized: a few weak FILL items, several questions/answers that re-explain prior-chapter concepts more than necessary, and a handful of stems or distractor sets that are more recall-oriented than ideal.

missed_buildup_opportunities.explanation: The question/answer treats Lighthouse Models partly as a re-introduction to bottleneck analysis without assuming prior familiarity with the iron law, even though the chapter and prior vocabulary allow stronger reuse of that earlier concept.

This question re-explains the additive vs. max-form distinction at a fairly introductory level. At chapter 2, readers can be assumed to know the iron law term from chapter 1, so the item could push more on application than re-framing.
The answer restates the bottleneck logic in very general terms rather than leaning on prior-chapter familiarity with bottleneck-style reasoning and moving faster to deployment-specific implications.
The FILL asks for the name of the principle directly rather than assuming the learner already knows the concept and applying it to this chapter's hardware context.

build_up.chapter_level_assessment: The chapter generally does build appropriately on prior context, especially around the AI Triad and the iron law. However, several questions still behave as if this were an isolated chapter, re-explaining previously introduced framing instead of using prior vocabulary as shared background.

distribution.type_mix_assessment: over-MCQ, under-SHORT, roughly on TF/FILL by count, under-ORDER

per_question_issues.description: The FILL blank is just 'memory,' which is too generic and tests surface completion more than conceptual inference. Multiple nearby words or formulations could cue it, and it does not really target a distinctive chapter concept.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a more chapter-specific inference target, such as SRAM, latency budget, or deployment paradigm, in a sentence where the surrounding context uniquely determines the term.

per_question_issues.description: The TF item is valid but a bit too easy because the stem strongly signals the falsehood with 'arbitrary product categories' and 'mainly because engineers chose.' A reader who absorbed the section's framing can reject it without much reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make the misconception more plausible by mixing physics with a partial truth, e.g. saying regulation and vendor ecosystems are the primary cause while physics is secondary.

per_question_issues.description: The Lighthouse Models question is mostly sound, but it stays at definition/role recall rather than leveraging prior-chapter iron-law vocabulary to ask how these models support bottleneck reasoning in context.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Reframe as an application question: ask which Lighthouse Model would best expose a memory-bound vs. compute-bound failure mode and why.

per_question_issues.description: This FILL is pure term recall ('memory wall') and does not require conceptual inference beyond a standard phrase completion. The surrounding clause practically gives away the answer.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Convert to MCQ or SHORT asking what system symptom follows from compute growing faster than memory bandwidth, or ask which optimization class becomes more valuable under the memory wall.

per_question_issues.description: The question is good but somewhat introductory for chapter 2: it re-teaches the additive/max distinction rather than asking the reader to apply the prior iron-law concept to a concrete deployment scenario.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Anchor it in a scenario, e.g. streamed inference vs. single request latency, and ask which formulation applies and what optimization follows.

per_question_issues.description: This FILL asks for 'Principle' in 'Bottleneck Principle,' which is effectively a title-completion exercise rather than systems reasoning. It also weakly re-tests naming of a concept already introduced earlier in the chapter and rooted in prior context.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a question that asks what happens when memory time exceeds compute time in a pipeline, or why overlapping stages makes the slowest resource dominant.

per_question_issues.description: Two distractors are notably weak: cloud as 'always the best choice for any real-time application' and cloud as eliminating the need to reason about bandwidth. Those are so directly opposed to the chapter's framing that the item becomes easier than intended.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Use more plausible distractors, such as 'cloud is best whenever privacy is not critical' or 'cloud is preferred whenever compute intensity exceeds local device limits, unless latency budgets are strict.'

per_question_issues.description: The Data Locality Invariant FILL is largely name recall; the stem already paraphrases the formal definition, so the learner just supplies the title. This underuses a rich section with strong worked examples.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask the learner to analyze a drone/video scenario and determine whether locality is mandatory, then justify using the transmission-vs-remote-response comparison.

per_question_issues.description: The TF statement is correct but too easy because published peak TOPS vs. sustained performance is a standard contrast stated explicitly and repeatedly in the section. It works more as recap than as misconception challenge.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Make the claim more nuanced, e.g. 'Peak NPU TOPS is a good predictor of short interactive bursts but not long-running workloads,' then require the learner to reason about thermal throttling.

per_question_issues.description: The SRAM FILL is better than some other FILLs because it is chapter-specific, but it still leans heavily on term recall and title-wording from the text instead of system reasoning.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Turn it into a SHORT or MCQ about why off-chip memory breaks TinyML energy budgets, asking for the mechanism and deployment consequence.

per_question_issues.description: The decision-framework-sequence question mainly checks memorization of the flowchart ordering rather than whether the reader can use that ordering on a scenario. It is acceptable but less valuable than the surrounding questions.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Replace with a scenario-based ordering item or MCQ where the learner chooses which constraint should be applied first and why the others come later.

per_question_issues.description: This asks for the label 'Progressive Deployment' from a direct description. It is correct, but it mostly tests memorized naming rather than understanding trade-offs among the three patterns.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Ask the learner to distinguish Progressive Deployment from Train-Serve Split in a concrete product architecture, requiring explanation of why one pattern fits better.

per_question_issues.description: The item is fine overall, but three choices are straightforward true lessons from the section, leaving the single false claim very exposed. The question becomes a recognition test of the explicitly labeled fallacy rather than a reasoning task.
per_question_issues.suggested_fix: Use distractors that are all plausible deployment beliefs, with one capturing the actual fallacy in a subtler form.
